sentences.text: Office.
I can't tell you exactly, the month.
But it was end '43.
And I worked there till May, '44.
The German had a paper on everybody has a little paper with his name, with his everything.
And even those people who die-- who were sent to crematoria, they had the remark there they-- on typhus, died.
On typhus, died, or heart attack, died, or this way, they're dead.
Because they thought in case after world war, they will be caught and couldn't dismiss the papers.
So people died.
They didn't do anything wrong.
On the end they did, when it finished, the camp.
I heard they burned the papers.
I didn't see it, no.
That-- that-- that is like a statistic, yeah?
Everybody has a-- [INAUDIBLE] there.
So we fill that, always, in.
And you have to write it with beautiful writing.
So I can write-- what's the name of this lady?
Helen?
Elena.
I was working with Elena in the office.
And she knew that writing, that beautiful writing.
And so I learned from her, that writing.
You know, that--
Hmm?
Calligraphy.
Yeah.
So and then, beginning of May, the news traveled that Hungarian Jewish women arrive-- are arriving.
But I didn't listen to that.
And suddenly, the woman commander, the [?
Mandl ?] called that she is choosing me to be the lageralteste that's in the new camp, C, for the Jewish arrivals.
So I said to Elena, I am so happy to be here in the office.
Why can't I stay here?
So-- but I couldn't tell that to the commander there.
So I said, excuse-- ah, I wouldn't want-- I wouldn't know how to be a logger, or this.
So she was laughing because she has teeth coming like that.
And she was laughing.
Ha ha, how could you be?
There wasn't an-- ever a camp like this.
But now you will be.
I did hundred excuses.
But nothing helped me.
And also, Katka, I said to her, why you want me?
She said, not only they want you.
But also, the underground want you.
Because they know that you will try to do the best.
Yes.
I don't know.
That's what she said.
They underground wants you.
Then I said, leave me alone.
I-- I-- so she said, not only they want you.
The underground want you.
Because they knew that I will speak up.
And so I did.
When the commander, Kramer-- was hanged in Krakow, after the war-- came to pick me up in a black car.
And he brought me to the camp C.
And he said, here you will be lageralteste.
And I looked in the-- around.
There was a barracks.
Empty.
Nothing in it.
So I said, here you want me to be lageralteste?
So the women will lie on the floor and then crawl one over the other?
And then in a few days you will call them Jewish swines?
Send them straight away to the crematory or other.
And he said-- because that's to say that.
And he looked at me up and down and said, what you care about others?
Care about your self!
He screamed.
And I said, Mr.
Lagerkommandant, if it would be in reverse and you would be in charge, and the prisoners would be German, and you wouldn't care about them, I would you call cold heart.
And then I stood and I thought oy, what did you say?
To whom did you say it?
[LAUGHS]
You know?
But I didn't budge.
Like I said, I just stood straight.
And he looked me like that.
OK.
So what you want?
And I said that, and that, and that, and that, and that, and that, and that.
OK.
Screamed at me.
OK, you will have it.
And from this moment, this commander of 6 foot 6 with hands like six hands, who used to always squash somebody by their neck who was, by the way, under his-- close to him, threw him down.
No, but everybody knew.
Because that was not once.
It was--
Hmm?
I did-- I didn't see it.
But people was telling me who saw it.
And when I told them that, what I told him.
So they said, you are lucky that he didn't take you by the neck like he did for others.
But from this moment, he had the respect for me.
And many things I could do, good for the people.
What sort of things?
Can you remember any examples?
[LAUGHS]
What good?
He told me that I have to watch the gate, the electric gate.
Because the women, some day, used to come to the electric wire and talk to the men who was in the man camp.
Here was the woman camp.
Here was the road.
And here was the man camp.
So they could talk over.
They even could throw over, things.
So he said, you are going to watch, they shouldn't talk together.
OK.
So I said to the young girl who was runner, when I am here, the women should talk there.
When I am coming-- there-- they shouldn't talk where I am.
And except that watch if a motorbike is coming.
Suddenly they said-- the runner said, a motorbike is coming.
So push all the people away.
So nobody should be here, only me.
So I am there, standing.
And he said, what are you doing here?
So I thought to myself, you made fool of me, saying you know that you told me to stand here.
But I said, you know what I'm doing here, and hey commander.
I am thinking that I want to go to the theater.
But I haven't got not soap, not lipstick, not perfume, not comb, not mirror, not stockings, not underwear, and not a beautiful dress.
And I thought to myself, what is he's going to answer?
And he said, you are right.
You can't go without that to a theater.
Tomorrow morning, you come to the door.
I will be waiting for you.
And I go out with you together, to fetch the things what you want.
So next morning I thought he-- he forgotten.
No.
They said, Lageralteste Magda, come to the front.
Here stands Kramer.
I said, but I need 50 people.
He didn't ask for what.
Have 50 people.
So here we watched-- we was walking in the front, the commander and me, and 50 people behind.
When we arrived to the place where they sorted out the goods which the arrivals brought.
So he said, wait here.
And I'm going to bring you everything, what you need.
So I quickly, to the next person, I said, get yourself those trolleys.
And pack them up, five trolleys.
So that always, 10 girls could carry it.
And pack them up with soap, medicaments, towels, blankets, jumpers, whatever.
[INAUDIBLE] full, all.
So quickly I said, get everybody to leave everywhere, to fill it because you won't be too long.
They filled it so big, the 10 girls, to every one.
And they were standing.
Here the commander arrive.
And he says, he bought a little cabinet which is a mirror, which can be moved this way and that way and had little two drawers.
And we opened the drawers and here he said, here you have the soap, the perfume, the lipstick, there.
Here you have the brush, the comb, the this, the that.
Here you have the underwear, the stockings, the that.
And here you have beautiful silver dress.
Are you satisfied?
I said very.
I want to carry it.
No.
I promised I get it for you.
I get it.
So here we again, go to the door, to the camp.
And the girls with the trolleys behind.
Of course he saw them.
So-- and I thought, what will happen now?
He says to the SS man, let them through.
And when they are empty, take them back with the girls, few girls, and take them back, where it comes from.
And he said, and now, I give it to you.
I brought it till the camp.
Now you can take it to your room.
And now you can go to the theater.
But what we had, not what I got, this.
But we had soap and medication and towels and jumpers and-- which we divided by-- I put, always, 50-- the-- the runners put 50 women.
And they came in.
And so slowly we divided all the things.
And so many people had help-- soap and hygiene and medication.
We left and that we gave to the doctors.
32,000 women.
Beg pardon?
I have assistant?
No.
I made it differently.
First of all, I established commandos.
Hygiene is very important.
A woman came to me from my home town.
I'm from your home town.
Could you help me?
I said, yes, you are going to help me.
You pick hundred woman and make sure the cleanliness in the camp is spotless.
The washrooms, the toilet, and everywhere.
I picked somebody for runners.

Because the camp was very [? wide. ?] If there is a message somewhere, there, I-- I-- I-- when I was picked to be lageralteste there, so I picked, again, some woman to be blockalteste for the 32 barracks from girls who was working outside the camp.
So again, to save them.
And they picked from the-- the people who arrived, people-- 16 people who helped them with the bread, deliveries also.
And then I-- I sat with the runner to run around.
And all the doctors should announce and come to me.
I divided them in every barrack, two or three accordingly.
And I said to them, to watch if a epidemic breaks out.
Then when there is, please come and tell me.
Not a long time it went.
And the epidemic, scarlet fever, came out.
And they said, what shall we do?
I said, there, in the back of the barrack, is a wash house.
Put them there.
And the blockalteste was there.
She asked and how will we with the role call?
I said, that, leave to me.
Because it was very hot.
It was probably June.
So this would be June, '40--
Must be June.
Very hot.
So I approach [?
Grazer, ?] who was the-- the commander of this camp.
I said, what should you run and do the counting?
Because I supposed to go with her anyway.
So I said, be here in the shade.
I will do the counting.
So she said, what will the woman commander says from other camp?
I said, are you the-- the commander of this camp?
Yes.
So what you care about-- do what is more comfortable for you.
You stay here, and also the report writer.
And I will bring the amount.
We will compare.
And if it's everything, all right, that's all right.
So that's how I did for six weeks.
Because that is the time, how long the scarlet fever last.
And after that, I let her do that.
But I went with her anyway.
So-- What would have happened if you had--
Beg pardon?
If I wouldn't done it and they would find out that scarlet fever is there, the epidemic there, they will take the 32,000 people to the crematoria.
How do I know about that?
Because opposite us was a small camp.
They called that Mexico.
They had 8,000 people, women there.
Their style wasn't so thinking of the good of the people.
And so they didn't do any precaution.
And then scarlet fever broke out.
And the SS found out.
They ordered the tracks.
They put the 8,000 women right to the gas chambers.
Because they was cross the road.
And sometimes they needed something from the big camp, so they came over.
And also, they was in touch with us because we knew those girls.
One of the-- who was in function.
In-- who was in charge.
She said, imagine what happened.
I said, what happened?
Scarlet fever broke out.
They found out.
And they took all the 8,000 people to the gas chamber.
I didn't tell her you should-- you should prevent it.
But I'm not here to preach others, what to do.
Nobody should know.
No.
Only the doctors.
And only the blockalteste.
Because if one woman, just by off chance would say, we've been in the back of the barrack because we had scarlet fever.
Well-- [LAUGHS]
So everything has to be very secretive.
Like, because you couldn't rely on-- maybe she will say something silly, not thinking about anything.
And one thing I tell you, one day came a doctor to the camp.
His name was Dr.
Klein.
He was Romanian, origin.
And he spoke Hungarian.
And he came and he started to gather around himself some girls.
They was young girls, beautiful girls.
But everybody likes the comfort.
And he said, why should you be here in that dirty place?
And I have a beautiful sanatorium.
You are beautiful girls.
I take you there.
There will be gardens and this and that.
So the runners run.
Magda, come quickly.
Because they are-- they are gathering and they are pushing themselves.
They all wants to go to the sanatorium.
So what can you do, quick?
You have to be a little bit tough.
So I said first, you are not going to go to the sanatorium.
Because if I can be in this bloody camp, so could you to.
And they still wanted to go.
So I said, here you have sanatorium.
Here you [? are. ?] And run, all of you, from here.
And I said to the doctor, Dr.
Klein, you don't belong here.
You have nothing to do here.
If I will tell that to the commander Kramer that you've been here, in that camp, where you haven't got-- then you-- he won't-- won't be pleased.
So you better move out.
I could say that because he's not going to really ask Kramer.
And also, I could afford because Kramer behaved to me differently like to others.
He had a certain respect for me.
Like what respect?
One day, a runner comes and says, Magda, come to the front.
The lagerkommandant wants you.
I came to the front.
He is sitting in a big, black car.
And this is before the SS man are there, in the boot.
And the lagerkommandant-- Irma Grese is there.
And he says, lageralteste, come sit next to me in the car.
And I said Lagerkommandant Kramer, please.
How will it look that I, Jewish, sitting next to the commander?
What will they think what connection we have together?
Here, Dr.
Mengele.
You heard of Dr.
Mengele.
Here he is.
Take him to the car.
Let him bloody walk!
And all the SS heard I want you to the car.
I have to discuss with you some matters.
So I said, you know what, [INAUDIBLE], I make a compromise.
That car had a-- like a step in the front.
Old cars had that.
New cars hasn't got.
I will stand on that Stand and I will lean on the car.
And that's how we will go to the camp.
What I forgot, the Warsaw Uprising, they heard about me.
And they sent-- and I don't know how they did that, because it's dangerous-- they send me from the uprising a ring, a memorabilia for-- from the uprising.
And I had that on my ring.
And because it was so quickly run, because-- so I didn't think to take it off.
So as my hand was here on the car.
And he said, what?
You're wearing the ring of the Warsaw Uprising before my eyes?
I take the revolver and shoot the whole hand off!
And he said, no, you don't even move your hand away?
I said, no.
You are the commander.
And you will do what you want anyway.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He did like that.
Oh, go away, he says.
And he put the revolver in.
And that all-- the commander and that-- all they saw, saw my prestige went higher.
And so many things I could do which I wouldn't be able to if I wouldn't be so outspoken, daring.
Because I said, only once I am going to the chimney.
But till I go, I do everything in the good of the people.
Even if it's dangerous.
I would let him take one?
To send them to the gas chambers.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Because-- because he was-- he didn't have any sanatorium.
He didn't have anything.
He wasn't a top doctor.
He was just [YIDDISH].
I was about at-- September, let's say, about six months, eh?
The camp finished.
And imagine, that shows you, the commander came to pick me up.
And he said, come with me.
Now I show you how hard you were going to work and how hard you will have it.
And I said, like all other Jews.
Now we will have it even harder.
So it's my fate.
I can't change it.
So-- And he was teasing me that till we arrived to the kitchen in Birkenau.
He came to the kitchen and there was the SS woman in charge.
And she said-- and he said, here I bring you the kapo of the kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I was thinking there is a kapo.
Because you know everything beforehand, when I was in Birkenau.
Kapo of the kitchen was a German woman, who was a lesbian friend of the lageralteste, of the German lageralteste.
The German lageralteste was very good to the Jews and sympathetic.
Now, if I take the position of her girlfriend, and she maybe have-- I don't know what position-- she will be bad to the Jews.
And I don't need that at all.
So I said, [?
Schulz ?] was her name, [?
Aufseherin ?] [?
Schulz. ?] You know what?
I have a proposition.
I am much better in counting the food, like the mathematic.
Counting the food, and I will be in the office.
And let her stay a kapo.
She is used to it.
But what will the commander say?
He will be satisfied.
If you think that he will be satisfied, that's all right.
So I got the-- in the office.
And I was in charge to count out the food.
How much that, and how much that.
The prisoners didn't know why their rations was doubled.
Because I now counting.
I counted everything double.
But I wasn't there too long.
I wasn't there too long.
Because suddenly, they called--
Ah, four weeks, about.
Maybe-- maybe not even four weeks.
Two weeks.
October, that was the beginning of October.
Say, in the middle of October.
Because I have to consider what happened.
So again, the lagerkommandant, with the black car.
And now I want you to sit in the car.
So now I am going to take you somewhere.
Hmm?
